coredata.dc:title: A sensitive electrochemical genosensor for measuring gastric cancer biomarker, microRNA-106b, based on asparagine polymerization on carbon quantum dots modified electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Kuche-Meshki, Mohammad
Zare, Hamid R.
Akbarnia, Azam
Mohammad Moshtaghioun, S.
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this work is to develop an electrochemical genosensor to measure microRNA 106b (miR-106b) as a gastric cancer biomarker. To construct the miR-106b genosensor, asparagine (As) was electropolymerized on the surface of carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode (CQDs/GCE). In the next step, ssDNA was immobilized on the modified electrode surface (As/CQDs/GCE) and then hybridized with miR-106b. Finally, differential pulse voltammograms of the accumulated chlorogenic acid (CGA), as an electroactive label, on the surface of immobilized ssDNA was recorded before (ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE) and after hybridization with miR-106b (miR-106b/BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE). The difference in the peak current of these two voltammograms was used as an analytical signal to measure of miR-106b. Using this genosensor, a linear concentration range from 1.0 fM to 1.0 µM and a detection limit of 0.39 fM were obtained for the measurement of miR-106b. The obtained data indicate that it is possible to recognize miR-106 from a non-complementary and a single-base mismatch sequence using the genosensor. In addition, the measurement of miR-106b in a human serum sample was successfully performed using the genosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Asparagine
Chlorogenic acid
MicroRNA 106b
Biomarkers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and electropolymerized asparagine (As)
Tracking the steps of making the desired genosensor to measure miR-106b by electrochemical methods
Optimum experimental conditions for genosensor construction
. Analytical performance of the genosensor for measuring miR-106b
. Selectivity, reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the genosensor
Investigating the application of the proposed genosensor to measure miR-106 in a human serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and (B, C) FESEM images of GCE modified with CQDs (CQDs/GCE) and asparagine electropolymerized on the CQDs/GCE (As/CQDs/GCE).

(A) Nyquist plots and (B) cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE, CQDs/GCE, As/CQDs/GCE, ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE and miR-106b/BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl. The inset of Fig. 2A shows the equivalent circuit used to draw the Nyquist plots.

Effect of different parameters (A) the EDC/NHS incubation time, (B) the probe immobilization time, (C) the probe concentration, (D) the target hybridization time, (E) the CGA concentration and (F) the CGA accumulation time on the current response of the genosensor. The error bars related to the relative standard deviation of three repeated measurements (n = 3).

Differential pulse voltammograms of CGA accumulated on the BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE surface before (a) and after hybridization with different concentrations of the complementary sequence in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). b–k represent concentrations of 1.0 µM to 1.0 fM of miR-106b, respectively. Inset (A) shows the redox reaction of chlorogenic acid, CGA. Inset (B) shows the difference of the current responses CGA accumulated on the surfaces of BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE (Ia) and miR-106b/BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE (Ib) (ΔI = Ia – Ib) vs. log [miR–106b]. Error bars correspond to the relative standard deviation of three replicate measurements (n = 3).

Differential pulse voltammograms of the ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE (a) and the CGA accumulated on the BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE surface before (b) and after hybridization with a 1.0 nM a complementary sequence (miR-106b) (c), a non-complementary sequence (d), and a single-base mismatch (e) in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0).
Schematic illustration of the construction of the electrochemical genosensor for the determination of miR-106b.
Comparison of the analytical parameters of different biosensors used to measure miR-106b and the other microRNAs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ssDNA: Single-stranded DNA, GQDs: Graphene quantum dots, PGE: pencil graphite electrode, PAA: Poly(acrylic acid), RGO: Reduced graphene oxide, GCE: Glassy carbon electrode, Au-PtBNPs: gold platinum bimetallic nanoparticles, CGO: Carboxylated graphene oxide, FTO: Fluorine tin oxide, GA: Glutaraldehyde, AGr: Amine-functionalized graphene, PCA: 1-pyrene carboxylic acid, AuNPs: Gold nanoparticles, CP: Capture probe, SPCE: Screen-printed carbon electrode, Cys-AuNPs: Cysteamine-capped gold nanoparticles, Au-SPE: Au-screen printed electrode, BSA: Bovine serum albumin, As: Asparagine, CQDs: Carbon quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of measurement and recovery miR-106b in a human serum sample (n = 3) using the proposed genosensor.

head.ce:title: A sensitive electrochemical genosensor for measuring gastric cancer biomarker, microRNA-106b, based on asparagine polymerization on carbon quantum dots modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yazd University, Yazd 89195–741, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Yazd University, Yazd 89195–741, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Yazd University, Yazd 89195-741, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biology, Yazd University, Yazd 89195-741, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the construction of the electrochemical genosensor for the determination of miR-106b.

The aim of this work is to develop an electrochemical genosensor to measure microRNA 106b (miR-106b) as a gastric cancer biomarker. To construct the miR-106b genosensor, asparagine (As) was electropolymerized on the surface of carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode (CQDs/GCE). In the next step, ssDNA was immobilized on the modified electrode surface (As/CQDs/GCE) and then hybridized with miR-106b. Finally, differential pulse voltammograms of the accumulated chlorogenic acid (CGA), as an electroactive label, on the surface of immobilized ssDNA was recorded before (ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE) and after hybridization with miR-106b (miR-106b/BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE). The difference in the peak current of these two voltammograms was used as an analytical signal to measure of miR-106b. Using this genosensor, a linear concentration range from 1.0 fM to 1.0 µM and a detection limit of 0.39 fM were obtained for the measurement of miR-106b. The obtained data indicate that it is possible to recognize miR-106 from a non-complementary and a single-base mismatch sequence using the genosensor. In addition, the measurement of miR-106b in a human serum sample was successfully performed using the genosensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are endogenous non-coding small RNA molecules with a length of about 18–25 nucleotides [1]. MiRNAs were discovered in 1993 [2,3], and their application as biomarkers was studied in 2002 [4]. They play an important role in various biological processes including cell cycle, proliferation, differentiation, apoptosis, metabolism, and drug resistance [1,5]. MiRNAs can regulate gene expression in normal and pathological cellular processes [6]. Circulating MiRNAs are a group of miRNAs released into the blood that can be assessed by blood sampling and subsequent molecular analysis [7]. Dysregulation of miRNA expression is directly related to cancer. Thus, a specific expression profile is formed that can be used to detect cancer at an early stage [5]. MiRNAs are introduced as non-invasive biomarkers for early detection, diagnosis, metastasis, prognosis, and treatment monitoring [7]. Expression patterns of miRNAs are related to the prognosis and outcome in cancer [8]. miR-106b has been identified as a novel biomarker for gastric cancer [9–11]. The expression level of miR-106b is increased in gastric cancer. Despite various research on miRNA, their determination in clinical samples is challenging due to their short length, high homolog sequence, and low abundance [12,13]. Various methods have been developed for sensitive, selective and reliable detection of miRNAs. The use of biosensors as analytical and diagnostic tools in detection of biomarkers has recently received attention. These devices work based on a specific interaction between the target and the biological element [14]. The high sensitivity, selectivity, simple operation and reasonable price of these devices have made them suitable candidates for measuring cancer biomarkers.

The use of nanotechnology can be effective in miniaturizing biosensors and improving their responses sensitivity [7]. In this regard, a wide range of nanomaterials have been used in the construction of biosensors used to determine miRNA [15–21]. Quantum dots (QDs) are a type of semiconductor nanomaterials with properties such as photostability, tunable particle sizes, high biocompatibility, and good electrical and chemical stability [22–24]. These properties have made these materials widely used in the construction of bioimaging [25,26], photocatalysis [27], drug delivery [28], photoinduced therapy [29], and biosensors [30,31].

Amino acids are able to form strong bonds with carboxylic and amine groups in other compounds due to having different functional groups in their structures. Therefore, in this study, asparagine (As), as an amino acid, has been used to make a surface rich in COOH functional group for bonding with the NH2-probe [32–34]. Chlorogenic acid (CGA) is a phenolic natural compound found in many fruits and vegetables. The positive effects of chlorogenic acid in the treatment of some diseases such as cardiovascular, diabetes and cancer have been confirmed [35–37]. In addition, CGA is an electroactive compound and it is claimed that the biological activities of such polyphenolic compounds are closely related to their electrochemical properties [38]. Therefore, in this research, CGA has been used as a new electroactive label in the construction of a biosensors.

The aim of this research is to investigate the possibility of using CQD as a substrate to make an electrochemical genosensor that can be used in the detection and measurement of miR-106b, as a gastric cancer biomarker. In this regard, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was first modified by CQD and then asparagine (As) was electropolymerized on it. CQD increases the surface area of the electrode and the asparagine polymer film increases the functional groups necessary to immobilize the single-stranded DNA (probe) on the modified electrode surface. Chlorogenic acid (CGA) has also been used as an electroactive indicator. The difference in the peak current of CGA accumulated on the modified electrode surface containing the immobilized probe, before and after hybridization with miR-106b was used as an analytical signal. The obtained data show that by using this genosensor, it is possible to measure miR-106b in a wide linear concentration range with a low detection limit. It is also possible to use the genosensor to measure miR-106b in human serum samples.

Chitosan, acetic acid, chlorogenic acid (CGA), asparagine (As), tris–hydroxymethylaminomethane hydrochloride (Tris), bovine serum albumin (BSA), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) −3-ethylcarbodiimide hydro-chloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), sodium hydroxide, phosphoric acid (H3PO4), and other reagents were obtained in analytical grades from Merck Company. Oligonucleotide sequences used to construct the genosensor listed below were purchased from Bioneer Corporation (South Korea).
NH2-Probe (ssDNA) sequence: 5ˊ–NH2–ATC TGC ACT GTC AGC ACT TTA–3ˊ.
miR-106b (target) sequence: 5ˊ–TAA AGT GCT GAC AGT GCA GAT–3ˊ.
One-base mismatch sequence: 5ˊ–TAA ACT GCT GAC AGT GCA GAT–3ˊ.
miR-200a (non-complementary) sequence: 5ˊ–CAT CTT ACC GGA CAG TGC TGG A–3ˊ.

Stock solutions of the oligonucleotides (100.0 μM) were prepared with 10.0 mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH 8.0) containing 1.0 mM of EDTA. A 1.0 M phosphate buffer (pH 4.5) was used to prepare the probe solution. Also, a 0.05 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing NaCl (0.3 M) was used to prepare the target solution and wash the electrode surface.

Electrochemical experiments were performed using an Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat model PGSTAT 30 (Eco chemic Utrecht, Netherlands) equipped with the General Purpose Electrochemical System Autolab (GPES) and Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) software. In the three-electrode system used, a modified glassy carbon electrode, a graphite rod, and an Ag/AgCl/KCl (sat’d) electrode were used as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. A pH/mV meter model 827 manufactured by Metrohm was used to measure the pH values. A field emission scanning electron microscope (MIRA3 TESCAN) operating at 15.0 kV was used to study the morphological structure of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and the asparagine film formed on CQDs. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were also recorded by a transmission electron microscope (Philips CM 120) operating at 120 kV.

Quantitative electrochemical measurements were carried out with differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) at a modulation amplitude of 25 mV and a step potential of 5 mV in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were performed in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– and 0.1 M KCl. A frequency range of 10 mHz-100 kHz and a potential of + 0.25 V were selected to record impedance data. Cyclic voltammograms were recorded with a potential scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and in the range of −200 to 700 mV.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using hydrothermal method. For this purpose, 0.25 g chitosan was dissolved in 50 mL of 1% acetic acid at room temperature using the ultrasonic device. Then 20 mL of 0.5% chitosan solution was transferred into a Teflon–lined stainless–steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the prepared solution was stored in a dark and clean container at 4 °C [39].

Before use, a bare GCE was polished with a 0.05 mm Al2O3 slurry on a polishing cloth and then rinsed. After cleaning, 5.0 μL of CQDs solution was dropped onto the GCE surface and allowed to completely dry and by physical adsorption of CQDs on the electrode surface, CQDs/GCE was formed. Next, asparagine (As) was electropolymerized on CQDs/GCE by ten cycles of potential scanning from −0.6 V to 1.6 V at 100 mV s−1 in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 20.0 mM of As to obtain As/CQDs/GCE [40]. The effective surface area of CQDs/GCE and unmodified GCE were estimated from cyclic voltammograms of 0.1 M KCl solution containing 1.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] at different potential scan rates (ν) and based on the Randles-Sevcik equation [41]. The results indicate that the effective surface area of CQDs/GCE and GCE are 0.042 cm2 and 0.030 cm2, respectively. These data show that the effective surface area of the modified electrode has increased by 40.0% compared to the unmodified electrode.

To activate the carboxylic groups on the surface of modified electrode, As/CQDs/GCE was immersed in EDC/NHS solution (1:10) for 45 min. Next, As/CQDs/GCE was incubated with 5.0 µL phosphate buffer (1.0 M, pH 4.5) containing 10.0 µM H2N-probe (ssDNA) in a humid environment for 90 min. The modified electrode prepared in this step is named as ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE. Then, the remaining active sites from the electrode surface, were not bound by the ssDNA, blocked by placing 5.0 μL of BSA solution (1% w/w) on it for 30 min (BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE). The genosensor (BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE) was incubated in the hybridization solution (0.05 M phosphate buffer + 0.3 M KCl, pH 7.0) containing different concentrations of the target solution (miR-106b) for 45 min. The prepared miR106b/BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE was then washed and immersed in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 8.0 mM chlorogenic acid (CGA) for 60 min. To investigate the effect of the hybridization process on CGA accumulation on the electrode surface, BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE was also immersed in a CGA solution (8.0 mM) for 60 min. Finally, the differential pulse voltammograms of CGA accumulated on BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE (Ia) and miR106b/BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE (Ib) in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) were recorded and the difference in their current response (ΔI = Ia – Ib) was considered as an analytical signal. Scheme 1 shows the details of the genosensor construction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and electropolymerized asparagine (As)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A shows the TEM image of the CQDs. As can be seen, CQDs have a spherical shape with a small size about less of 20 nm. SEM images of the CQDs modified GCE before and after electropolymerization of asparagine (As) on its surface are also shown in Fig. 1B and 1C. The spherical structure of CQDs can also be seen in Fig. 1B. Fig. 1C shows an electrodeposited polymer film of As on the CQDs/GCE surface. In the absence of the polymer, individual quantum dots are visible, while a porous network is formed after electropolymerization. This polymerized film is provided a suitable substrate for immobilization the probe on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tracking the steps of making the desired genosensor to measure miR-106b by electrochemical methods
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of the genosensor during stepwise modification was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) methods. Fig. 2 shows the Nyquist plots and the cyclic voltammograms of the genosensor after each preparation step in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl. Nyquist plots of different steps of modification are shown in Fig. 2A. As it can be observed, GCE shows a little resistant (Rct = 0.79 kΩ). By modification of GCE with CQDs, the semicircle diameter increased. The semiconducting property of CQDs and the presence of functional carboxyl groups with negative charge on the surface of CQDs led to an increase in the value of Rct (Rct = 1.76 kΩ) and, a result, a decrease in electron transfer rate between the redox probe and the modified electrode surface. As expected, with the electropolymerization of As on the modified electrode surface, the of Rct increased (Rct = 2.32 kΩ). In fact, the polymer film on the electrode surface acts as a blocking layer and decreases the access of the redox probe to the modified electrode surface and thus increase the value of Rct. By immobilizing the probe (ssDNA) on the modified electrode surface, the Rct value increases further (Rct = 3.03 kΩ). This increase is caused by the electrostatic repulsion between the negative charges of the ssDNA backbone on the electrode surface and the redox probe. The semicircle diameter increases again after hybridization of the target with the immobilized probe (Rct = 4.25 kΩ). This can be attributed to more negative charges and a greater blocking effect on the electrode surface due to the formation of double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). The inset of Fig. 2A shows the equivalent circuit for the Nyquist plots. As shown in Fig. 2B, the results obtained from the cyclic voltammograms are in good agreement with the data obtained from the EIS plots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum experimental conditions for genosensor construction
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity, stability and selectivity of a genosensor can be affected by various parameters. Based on this, some important parameters including the EDC/NHS incubation time, the probe concentration, the probe immobilization time, the target hybridization time, the CGA concentration and the CGA accumulation time were optimized. To optimize the incubation time of EDC/NHS, the GCE was modified with CQDs and As. After that, the modified electrode was immersed in EDC/NHS solution (10:1) for different time periods (15, 30, 45, and 60 min). Then, 5.0 µL probe (10.0 µM) was placed on the modified electrode surface and incubated for 3 h. Next, the modified electrode surface was blocked by BSA and then exposed to a 10.0 mM CGA solution for 30 min. Fig. 3 A indicates that the accumulated CGA current response increased with the increment of the EDC/NHS incubation time up to 45 min. This time was therefore considered as the optimal time for the EDC/NHS incubator. The immobilization time of the probe was also optimized. For this purpose, 5.0 µL of the probe (10.0 µM) was placed on the As/CQDs/GCE surface and incubated for different periods from 60 to 150 min. After blocking the surface by BSA, CGA (10.0 mM) was accumulated on the modified electrode surface and the corresponding differential pulse voltammograms were recorded. As Fig. 3B shows, the best response is obtained for an immobilization time of 90 min. Accordingly, 90 min was chosen as the optimal immobilization time for subsequent experiments. The probe concentration was also optimized by placing different concentrations of the amino-modified probe (5.0–15.0 µM) on the As/CQDs/GCE surface and incubating for 90 min. After exposure to a CGA solution (10.0 mM) for 30 min, the resulting differential pulse voltammograms were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3C, the maximum current response was obtained when the probe concentration was 10.0 µM. Therefore, this concentration was selected in subsequent studies. To optimize the target hybridization time, a GCE was modified by CQDs and As, and then 5.0 µL probe (10.0 µM) was immobilized on the modified electrode surface for 90 min. The electrode surface was blocked by placing BSA on the modified electrode surface and then 1.0 pM of miR-106b was incubated on it for 30–75 min. Then it was placed in a 10.0 mM CGA solution for 30 min. Fig. 3D shows the plot of the accumulated CGA current response versus the hybridization time. The data in this figure show that the current response increased with increasing the hybridization time up to 45 min. Thus, 45 min was chosen as the optimal hybridization time. The concentration and the accumulation time of CGA were also optimized. To optimize the CGA concentration, the probe (10.0 µM) was immobilized on the As/CQDs/GCE. Then it was placed in different concentrations of CGA (4.0–12.0 mM) for 30 min. Fig. 3E shows the current response increased with enhancement the CGA concentration up to 8.0 mM and then remained nearly constant. In order to investigate the effect of the CGA accumulation time on the current response, BSA/ssDNA/CQDs/GCE was placed in 8.0 mM CGA solution from 30 to 75 min. As seen in Fig. 2E, the best current response was obtained at 60 min. Based on the results, 8.0 mM and 60 min were selected as the optimal concentration and accumulation time for the CGA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: . Analytical performance of the genosensor for measuring miR-106b
ce:section.ce:para:
The genosensor efficiency for the quantitative measurement of miR-106b was studied by the DPV method under optimal conditions. The differential pulse voltammograms of the accumulated CGA after hybridization of BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE with different concentrations of miR-106b are shown in Fig. 4 . The data in this figure indicate that the accumulated CGA peak currents decrease with increasing miR-106b concentration. Inset (A) of this figure shows the redox reaction of CGA [42]. Plot of the difference in peak current corresponding to accumulated CGA at the surfaces of BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE (Ia) and miR-106b/BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE (Ib) (ΔI = Ia – Ib) versus the logarithm of miR-106b concentration is depicted in the inset (B) of Fig. 4. The data in this figure show that the values of ΔI decreases linearly with the increase of the logarithm of miR-106b concentration from 1.0 fM to 1.0 µM. To estimate the detection limit of the proposed genosensor, the average current responses (ybl) and the standard deviation (sbl) were determined by repeated measurements in a blank sample. Then, using the equation yl.o.d = ybl + 3sbl [43] and the calibration plot, the detection limit (DL) of miR-106b was estimated to be 0.39 fM. The performance of the proposed genosensor is also compared with some other biosensors in Table 1
[44–50]. The data in this table denotes that the performance of this genosensor is acceptable and comparable to the other biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: . Selectivity, reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the genosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of a biosensor, that is, the recognition of a specific target sequence from other sequences, is a key factor in the design and development of biosensors. To study the selectivity of the proposed genosensor, BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE was hybridized with a complementary sequence (miR-106b), a non-complementary sequence, and a single-base mismatch sequence. CGA was then accumulated on the surfaces of these modified electrodes and their electrochemical current responses were recorded. Differential pulse voltammograms of ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE, without any CGA accumulated (a) and CGA accumulated on BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE before (b) and after hybridization with (c) a complementary sequence (miR-106b), (d) a non-complementary sequence, and (e) a single-base mismatch are shown in Fig. 5 . The voltammograms in this figure show that the current response of CGA accumulated on the modified electrode surface is significantly decreased after hybridization of BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE with the complementary sequence. Incubation of BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE with non-complementary DNA sequence did not significantly change in the current response of the accumulated CGA, indicating that no hybridization occurred between the non-complementary sequence and immobilized probe. However, when BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE was incubated with the single-base mismatch DNA sequence, a small decrease in the accumulated CGA current response occurred. Furthermore, the large difference between the responses of BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE before (voltammogram a) and after (voltammogram d) hybridization with the target indicates that CGA, as an electrochemical indicator, can distinguish single-stranded sequence from double-stranded ones. It is also possible to distinguish the single-base mismatch sequence from the complementary sequence (miR-106b).

The reproducibility of the genosensor was also investigated by preparing four BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE independently and measuring 0.1 nM miR-160b by using these biosensors. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for four reiterative measurements with the prepared genosensors was 7.2% (n = 4). To evaluate the repeatability of the genosensor, 0.1 nM miR-106b was incubated on a BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE and repeated measurements were performed on it. In the repeated measurements, the RSD value was 4.2% (n = 4). To investigate the stability of the proposed genosensor, a BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE was prepared and stored at 4 °C for four days. After this time, the response of the genosensor reached about 97% of the initial response. These results suggest that the proposed genosensor has satisfactory reproducibility, repeatability and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the application of the proposed genosensor to measure miR-106 in a human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: In this step, the prepared genosensor was used to measure miR-106b in a human serum sample. First, a healthy non-cancerous human serum sample was prepared from a clinical laboratory. The serum sample was diluted 10 times with a phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). Then different concentrations of miR-106b were spiked to the diluted serum sample. In the next step, each time on of the solutions prepared with different concentrations of miR-106b was incubated on a newly prepared BSA/ssDNA/As/CQDs/GCE. Finally, the differential pulse voltammograms of the accumulated CGA on the genosensor surface were recorded in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). Table 2 shows the results of three repeated measurements for each concentration. In this assay, the recovery rates of 95.1%–108.0% and the relative standard deviations of 1.7%–4.6% were obtained for the measurement of miR-106b. These results indicate the good performance of the discussed genosensor for measuring miR-106b in real samples.

The results of this research indicate that by deposition carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and electropolymerization of asparagine (As) on the surface of an electrode and using chlorogenic acid as an electroactive label, a sensitive and selective genosensor can be prepared to measure miR-106b as a biomarker of gastric cancer. Using this genosensor and the differential pulse voltammetry method, a wide linear concentration range of 1.0 fM to 1.0 µM and a low detection limit were obtained for the determination of miR-106b. The results show that by the proposed genosensor, a complementary sequence can be distinguished from a single-base mismatch sequence and a complementary sequence. In addition, the proposed genosensor is able to measure miR-106b in human serum samples with satisfactory results.

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are endogenous non-coding small RNA molecules with a length of about 18–25 nucleotides. The aim of this research is to investigate the possibility of using CQD and electropolymerized asparagine as a composite to make an electrochemical genosensor that can be used in the detection and measurement of miR-106b, as a gastric cancer biomarker. In this regard, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was first modified by CQD and then asparagine (As) was electropolymerized on it. CQD increases the surface area of the electrode and the asparagine polymer film increases the functional groups necessary to immobilize the single-stranded DNA (probe) on the modified electrode surface. Chlorogenic acid (CGA) has also been used as an electroactive indicator. The difference in the peak current of CGA accumulated on the modified electrode surface containing the immobilized probe, before and after hybridization with miR-106b was used as an analytical signal. The obtained data show that by using this genosensor, it is possible to measure miR-106b in a wide linear concentration range with a low detection limit. The obtained data indicate that it is possible to recognize miR-106 from a non-complementary and a single-base mismatch sequence using the genosensor. In addition, the measurement of miR-106b in a human serum sample was successfully performed using the genosensor.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
